envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Secondary Smelting, Refining, and Alloying of Nonferrous Metal (except Copper and Aluminum)

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue combines toll or treatment charges, recovery-value sharing, and sales of ingot, billet, alloy, powder, or other primary forms, generally under recurring feedstock and offtake relationships with metal-price formulas.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes outsourced recovery/toll processing with commodity metal production from owned scrap. The lens retains repeat external recovery, refining, and alloying relationships because speculative and captive operations are not coherent recurring services.
lens.excluded_activities: Copper and aluminum recyclers, scrap brokers and sorting facilities without smelting or refining, captive internal recovery operations, speculative inventory vehicles, dormant assets, project shells, and non-control interests.
lens.included_activities: Independent US secondary smelters, refiners, and alloyers that repeatedly recover lead, nickel, cobalt, precious, tin, titanium, zinc, or other nonferrous metals from customer or contracted scrap streams, or toll-alloy purchased metal, with transferable operations in the target band.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data cover broader NAICS 331400, including primary smelting, rolling, and drawing, rather than 331492 secondary processing. I assign AI exposure to feedstock documentation, assay reconciliation, scheduling, procurement, maintenance knowledge, environmental records, quality certificates, sales, and administration, and low direct exposure to furnace operation, material preparation, sampling, and maintenance. Secondary plants may carry more compliance and feedstock-variability work but also more physical sorting and handling, leaving the net proxy direction uncertain.
caveats: Four-digit occupation data mix primary and secondary processes.
Current automation in assay labs, furnace controls, sorting, and emissions monitoring is not observed.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across secondary lead, battery-material, precious-metal, and mixed nonferrous plants, including existing laboratory, ERP, and process automation, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The broader industry is 53.18% production and 7.66% installation, maintenance, and repair. Secondary smelting requires scrap preparation, furnace and kettle operation, refining, alloying, sampling, dust control, and physical material movement. Information-heavy assay, settlement, planning, maintenance, compliance, and commercial tasks form a modest minority of wage-weighted work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects broader NAICS 331400 real output and USGS reports economy-wide scrap recycling and new facilities, not constant-quality demand for eligible 331492 toll processors. I combine the broader 2.4% annual output projection with observed expansion in critical-metal recycling, moderating the base because new hydrometallurgical technologies, commodity cycles, and project failures may displace incumbent smelting. Broader output could overstate mature lead/tin segments or understate battery-material recovery.
caveats: USGS totals include metals and recycling routes outside this NAICS.
Battery and electronics recovery may use processes or classifications different from traditional secondary smelting.
d5.change_evidence: Contracted feedstock and offtake volumes, announced capacity with commissioning status, end-market demand by metal, scrap generation forecasts, import/export flows, and incumbent plant utilization would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: USGS estimates $46 billion of old scrap was domestically recycled in 2025 and reports new US plants recovering nickel, tin, precious metals, titanium powder, and battery materials, with additional projects under construction. DOE documents strong demand and investment for domestic critical-material recovery. These forces support growth, tempered by volatile feedstock, regulation, exports, and technology substitution.

caveats: Public data do not distinguish treatment services from commodity metal sales.
Metal-price pass-through can inflate receipts and distort the injected EBITDA-band bridge.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of toll versus principal metal revenue, recurring feedstock contracts, ownership, captive status, normalized EBITDA, permit history, liabilities, and management independence would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Treatment-charge, recovery-sharing, and toll-alloy relationships can qualify as repeat outsourced processing. Eligibility is reduced by facilities that principally buy and speculate on scrap, captive recovery, integrated corporate plants, environmental liabilities, single-feedstock dependence, and firms without standalone management. The injected n uses size-class and margin assumptions rather than observed EBITDA.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 331492 ($894,803,267) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 331492 ($8,815,567,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $963,871 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 331492); class <5: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,785,455 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $350,306 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,656,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,109,854 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,499,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,648,635 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,392,109 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,905,932 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $149,263,550 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,285,509 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $168,385,450 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,037,225 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 81

caveats: Operator requirement is distinct from labor intensity.
Technology substitution among physical recovery routes belongs partly in d5.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on closed-loop customer-owned recycling, distributed autonomous hydrometallurgy, direct reuse without refining, and permit-light alternative processes would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Recovered metal requires physical receiving, preparation, smelting or refining, alloying, sampling, emissions control, waste handling, certification, and shipment by an accountable permitted operator. Software can improve routing, settlement, and control support but cannot supply recovered metal independently. Limited downside reflects alternate recycling technologies or customer insourcing, not pure software self-service.

caveats: No source directly measures pass-through of AI-derived savings.
Accounting margins can move with metal inventories even when processing economics are unchanged.
q.change_evidence: Feedstock and offtake contracts, payables and treatment formulas, assay settlement histories, price resets, recovery guarantees, and contribution margins excluding metal value would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Treatment charges, recovery sharing, and metal-index formulas can isolate some processing economics and allow retention between repricings. Sophisticated feedstock suppliers and offtakers negotiate expected recoveries, payables, and conversion charges, so visible efficiency gains are shared. Commodity price and assay volatility can swamp labor effects, while specialized hazardous streams and scarce permits support retention.

caveats: No six-digit AI implementation study was located.
Emerging battery-recycling processes may be more digital than mature secondary lead plants.
rho.change_evidence: Plant pilots measuring reconciliation labor, assay disputes, accepted work instructions, downtime, recovery yield, off-spec lots, safety incidents, and emissions deviations would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI can assist feedstock classification, assay and settlement reconciliation, production scheduling, maintenance retrieval, compliance records, quality documentation, and customer communication within five years. Variable scrap chemistry, hazardous operations, chain-of-custody, laboratory validation, environmental controls, legacy systems, and accountable release constrain implementation. Direct control of furnaces and emissions systems is excluded from the base.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures planned five-year sales or transfers among US employer-business owners generally, not completed transfers of secondary smelters. I discount its 22% employer intention rate for permitting, remediation exposure, feedstock contracts, working capital, and technical-owner dependence that can prevent qualifying transfers. Intentions likely overstate completed control changes.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not realized transaction probabilities.
Corporate battery-recycling projects and strategic joint ventures may fall outside the LMM control lens.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age data and five years of closed control transfers, closures, bankruptcies, and permit ownership changes from PitchBook, Capital IQ, EPA/state records, and specialist recyclers' advisers would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: General owner aging supports transfer flow, but hazardous-material facilities require diligence on permits, emissions, waste, closure obligations, assays, and feedstock continuity. Some exits become shutdowns or asset sales rather than transferable going concerns.

principal_driver: Domestic critical-material recycling investment and repeat feedstock/offtake contracts can support growing, operator-required processing demand.
principal_weakness: Environmental liabilities and metal-price-distorted economics sharply reduce the pool of safely transferable recurring-service firms.
